content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 16, 1994

Karen Calvert
Member
Mendocino County Planning Commission
P.O. Box 70
Albion, CA 95410

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-94-263

Dear Ms. Calvert:

This is in response to your request for advice concerning
Section 84308 of the Political Reform Act.¹

QUESTIONS

1. Does Section 84308 prohibit you, a Mendocino County
Planning Commissioner, from soliciting campaign contributions for
a candidate for the Secretary of State's Office?

2. Are there any limits applicable to you if you make
personal contributions to candidates for county or state offices?

CONCLUSIONS

1. You are prohibited from accepting, soliciting, or
directing a contribution of more than $250 from any party, or his
or her agent, or from any participant, or his or her agent, while
a proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for
use is pending before the planning commission and for three months
following the date a final decision is rendered in the proceeding.
Thus, this limitation applies if you accept, solicit, or direct
contributions on behalf of a candidate, such as a candidate for
the state office of Secretary of State and the person making the
contribution is a party, participant or an agent of a party or
participant in the proceeding.

Moreover, Section 84308 requires you to disqualify yourself
from voting on matters that come before the planning commission if

___________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-94-263
Page 2


you have accepted a contribution of more than $250 from a party or
participant during the past 12 months. You would not be required
to disqualify yourself from matters by virtue of "soliciting" or
"directing" a contribution for a candidate for the state office of
Secretary of State.

2. The Act provides no special limits applicable to a
planning commissioner's use of personal funds to make campaign
contributions. However, the contribution limits of Proposition 73
are applicable in special elections and special runoff elections.
Moreover, local contribution limits may be applicable.

FACTS

You serve on the Mendocino County Planning Commission as an
appointed member and as an "at large" representative. You are
interested in serving on a host committee and also soliciting
funds for a candidate for the position of California Secretary of
State in the November election.

ANALYSIS

In 1983, Section 84308 was added to the Act. The purpose for
the provision was to ensure that appointed members of boards or
commissions would not be biased by large campaign contributors or
potential contributors who might appear before them in a
proceeding involving a license, permit or entitlement for use.

Section 84308 applies to all appointed officers of any state
agency or local government agency, with the exception of the
courts or any agency in the judicial branch of government, the
Legislature, the Board of Equalization, and constitutional
officers. (Section 84308(a)(3).) This would include persons
appointed to the Mendocino County Planning Commission.

Your questions concern possible restrictions placed upon you
for soliciting and making contributions.

Section 84308

Section 84308 imposes two requirements on officers subject to
the section which may be applicable to you.

1. Disqualification

Section 84308(c) provides:

Prior to rendering any decision in a
proceeding involving a license, permit or other
entitlement for use pending before an agency, each
officer of the agency who received a contribution
within the preceding 12 months in an amount of more
than two hundred fifty dollars ($250) from a party
or from any participant shall disclose that fact on

Our File No. A-94-263
Page 3


     the record of the proceeding.  No officer of an
agency shall make, participate in making, or in any
way attempt to use his or her official position to
influence the decision in a proceeding involving a
license, permit, or other entitlement for use
pending before the agency if the officer has
willfully or knowingly received a contribution in
an amount of more than two hundred fifty dollars
($250) within the preceding 12 months from a party
or his or her agent, or from any participant, or
his or her agent if the officer knows or has reason
to know that the participant has a financial
interest in the decision, as that term is described
with respect to public officials in Article 1
(commencing with Section 87100) of Chapter 7.

                                    (Emphasis added.)

     Thus, if an officer has in fact accepted a contribution of
more than $250 during the last 12 months from a party or
participant in a proceeding involving a license, permit or other
entitlement for use pending before an agency, the officer must
disclose that fact on the record of the proceeding and must
disqualify himself or herself from participating.  (Section
84308(c).)  A person "accepts" or "receives" a contribution only
if the contribution is for that person's own candidacy or own
controlled committee.  (Regulation 18438.6(a); Harron Advice
Letter, No. A-86-189.)


     Under the facts you have presented, the contributions are for
another person's candidacy.  Thus, Section 84308(c), by its
express terms, would not apply.  (Nimr Advice Letter,
No. I-86-320.)

2.  Contribution Limitation

     Your primary question concerns Section 84308(b).  Section
84308(b) provides:

          No officer of an agency shall accept, solicit,
or direct a contribution of more than two hundred
fifty dollars ($250) from any party, or his or her
agent, or from any participant, or his or her
agent, while a proceeding involving a license,
permit, or other entitlement for use is pending
before the agency and for three months following
the date a final decision is rendered in the
proceeding if the officer knows or has reason to
know that the participant has a financial interest,
as that term is used in Article 1 (commencing with
Section 87100) of Chapter 7.  This prohibition
shall apply regardless of whether the officer
accepts, solicits, or directs the contribution for
himself or herself, or on behalf of any other

Our File No. A-94-263
Page 4


officer, or on behalf of any candidate for office
or on behalf of any committee.

Emphasis added.

Thus, you may not accept, solicit, or direct a contribution
of more than $250, from any party,2 or his or her agent, or from
any participant,3 or his or her agent, while a proceeding
involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for use is
pending before the planning commission and for three months
following the date a final decision is rendered in the
proceeding.4 (Section 84308(b).) Thus, this prohibition applies
if you accept, solicit, or direct a contribution on behalf of a
candidate for the state office of Secretary of State and the
person making the contribution is a party, participant or an agent
of a party or participant in the proceeding.

a. Total Amount Solicited

You ask whether you may solicit contributions so long as the
contributions do not exceed $250. The express provisions of the
statute prohibit solicitations of more than $250. (Section
84308(b).) Thus, you may solicit contributions on behalf of the
candidate provided the total amount from a party, participant, or
agent of a party or participant does not exceed $250.


2 "Party" means any person who files an application for, or is
the subject of, a proceeding involving a license, permit, or other
entitlement for use. (Section 84308(a)(1).)

3 "Participant" means any person who is not a party but who
actively supports or opposes a particular decision in a proceeding
involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for use and who
has a financial interest in the decision. A person actively
supports or opposes a particular decision in a proceeding if he or
she lobbies in person the officers or employees of the agency,
testifies in person before the agency, or otherwise acts to
influence officers of the agency. (Section 84308(a)(2).)

4 With respect to a participant, the officer must know or have
reason to know the participant has a financial interest in the
decision. An officer knows or has reason to know that a
participant has a financial interest in the decision in a
proceeding if facts are revealed in his or her written or oral
support or opposition before the agency which make the person's
financial interest apparent. In addition, an officer knows, or
should have known, about a proceeding pending before the agency if
either the officer has actual knowledge of the proceeding or has
received notice of the license, permit or other entitlement
proceeding. Notice includes receipt of an agenda or docket
identifying the proceeding and the party or other persons affected
by name. (Regulation 18438.7(a) and (b).)

Our File No. A-94-263
Page 5


b.     Solicitation Defined

Regulation 18438.6 provides in pertinent part:

     (c)  An officer "solicits" a contribution only
if he or she knows or has reason to know that the
person being solicited for a contribution is a
party or agent of a party, or is a participant or
agent of a participant and either:

          (1)  The officer or candidate personally
requests a contribution for his or her own
campaign or controlled committee, or for any
other candidate, public official or committee,
either orally or in writing; or

          (2)  The agent of the officer or
candidate with the officer or candidate's
knowledge, requests a contribution for the
officer or candidate's own campaign or
controlled committee.

     (d)  Notwithstanding subsection (c), a
solicitation does not include a request made in a
mass mailing sent to members of the public, to a
public gathering, or published in a newspaper, on
radio or TV, or in any other mass media.  A person
does not "solicit" solely because his or her name
is printed with other names on stationery or a
letterhead used to request contributions.

     (e)  A person "directs" a contribution if he
or she acts as the agent of another person or of a
committee other than his or her own controlled
committee in accepting a contribution on behalf of,
or transmitting a contribution to, such other
person or committee.

You have stated that you wish to host a committee and solicit
funds for a candidate for the state's office of Secretary of
State.  Certainly, a planning commissioner who is a member of a
host committee raising funds for a candidate is "soliciting"
contributions within the meaning of Section 84308. (Hiltachk
Advice Letter, No. A-92-636.)

c.     Solicitation by Spouse

You also ask if your spouse is similarly restricted from
soliciting funds.  Regulation 18438.6(c) limits this restriction
to contributions personally solicited by an official.  In the Nimr
Advice Letter (supra) we advised:

     A planning commissioner who serves on a
committee supporting a ballot measure and who

Our File No. A-94-263
Page 6


raises, solicits, or directs funds for the measure
could violate Section 84308(b) under certain
circumstances. If a commissioner solicits
contributions of two hundred fifty dollars ($250)
or more[5] from a person whom the commissioner
knows or has reason to know is a party, a
participant or an agent of a party or participant,
while a use entitlement proceeding is pending, the
commissioner would be violating Section 84308(b).
The violation would occur if the request for funds
was made directly or through an agent of the
commissioner. (Section 84308(b); Regulation
18438.6(c).)

Emphasis added.

Thus, if your spouse acts as your agent and solicits
contributions of more than $250 from a person you know or have
reason to know is a party, a participant, or an agent of a party
or participant, a prohibited solicitation will result.

d.   Aggregation of Contributions Under Section 84308

You also seek general guidance to determine when
contributions solicited must be aggregated for purposes of Section
84308.

As a general rule, to determine whether a contribution of
$250 or more has been made by a person6 or his or her agent,/
contributions made by that person within the preceding 12 months
must be aggregated with those made by his or her agent within the
preceding 12 months, or the period of the agency relationship,
whichever is shorter. (Regulation 18438.3(b).) In addition, we
have advised that if the same person directs and controls the
contributions of two entities, their contributions are aggregated

5    In 1989, Section 84308 was amended to apply only to
contributions of "more than two hundred fifty dollars ($250)."

6    A "person" means an individual, proprietorship, firm,
partnership, joint venture, syndicate, business trust, company,
corporation, association, committee, and any other organization or
group of persons acting in concert.

7    A person is the "agent" of a party to, or a participant in, a
proceeding involving a license, permit or other entitlement for
use only if he or she represents that person in connection with
the proceeding involving the license, permit or other entitlement
for use. If an individual acting as an agent is also an
employee or member of a law, architectural, engineering or
consulting firm, or a similar entity or corporation, both the
entity or corporation and the individual are "agents."
(Regulation 18438.3(a).)

Our File No. A-94-263
Page 7


for purposes of the $250 contribution limit imposed by Section
84308. (Breckenridge Advice Letter, No. I-94-126.)

An officer knows, or should have known about a contribution
if the contribution has been disclosed by the party (pursuant to
Section 84308(d)) or the officer has actual knowledge of the
contribution. (Regulation 18438.7(c).)

Limits Applicable to Personal Contributions

Finally, you ask if there are special limits on your personal
contributions to candidates for county or state office because you
are an appointed county planning commissioner. The Act provides
no special limits applicable to a planning commissioner's personal
funds.

In light of the decision in Service Employees International
Union, AFL-CIO, et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission (9th
Cir. 1992) 955 F.2d 1312, cert. denied, ___ U.S. ___, 112 S.Ct.
3056 ("SEIU"), the contribution limits which were added by
Proposition 73 in June of 1988 have been invalidated. However,
the court order did not invalidate the special election and
special runoff election contribution limits of Proposition 73.
Therefore, these restrictions remain operative in both local and
statewide special elections. (Section 85305.)⁸

Moreover, local valid contribution limits are still
applicable. Therefore, you should consult your county counsel
with respect to local contribution limits which may affect your
ability to make personal contributions to local candidates.

I trust this answers your questions sufficiently. If you
have additional questions, please feel free to contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division


8 The following contribution limitations apply during special
election cycles and special runoff election cycles: (1) $1,000
for persons; (2) $2,500 for political committees; and (3) $5,000
for broad based political committees or political parties.

limits this restriction

or the period of the agency relationship,
whichever is shorter.

local valid contribution limits are still
applicable.
Therefore,
you should consult your county counsel
with respect to local contribution limits which may affect your

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1.
Does Section 84308 prohibit you,
a Mendocino County
Planning Commissioner,
from soliciting campaign contributions for
a candidate for the Secretary of State’s Office?
2.
Are there any limits applicable to you if you make
personal contributions to candidates for county or state offices?

CONCLUSION: 1.
You are prohibited from accepting, soliciting,
or
directing a contribution of more than $250 from any party,
or his
or her agent,
or from any participant,
or his or her agent, while
a proceeding involving a license, permit,
or other entitlement for
use is pending before the planning commission and for three months
following the date a final d.pision is rendered in the proceeding.
Thus, this limitation applies if you accept, solicit,
or direct
contributions on behalf of a candidate,
such as a candidate for
the state office of Secretary of State and the person making the
contribution is a party, participant or an agent of a party or
participant in the proceeding.
Moreover,
Section 84308 requires you to disqualify yourself
from voting on matters that come before the planning commission if
et seq.

embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission September 16, 1994 Karen Calvert Member Mendocino County Planning Commission P.O. Box 70 Albion, CA 95410 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-94-263 Dear Ms. Calvert: This is in response to your request for advice concerning Section 84308 of the Political Reform Act.¹ QUESTIONS 1. Does Section 84308 prohibit you, a Mendocino County Planning Commissioner, from soliciting campaign contributions for a candidate for the Secretary of State's Office? 2. Are there any limits applicable to you if you make personal contributions to candidates for county or state offices? CONCLUSIONS 1. You are prohibited from accepting, soliciting, or directing a contribution of more than $250 from any party, or his or her agent, or from any participant, or his or her agent, while a proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for use is pending before the planning commission and for three months following the date a final decision is rendered in the proceeding. Thus, this limitation applies if you accept, solicit, or direct contributions on behalf of a candidate, such as a candidate for the state office of Secretary of State and the person making the contribution is a party, participant or an agent of a party or participant in the proceeding. Moreover, Section 84308 requires you to disqualify yourself from voting on matters that come before the planning commission if ___________________________ 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660 Our File No. A-94-263 Page 2 you have accepted a contribution of more than $250 from a party or participant during the past 12 months. You would not be required to disqualify yourself from matters by virtue of "soliciting" or "directing" a contribution for a candidate for the state office of Secretary of State. 2. The Act provides no special limits applicable to a planning commissioner's use of personal funds to make campaign contributions. However, the contribution limits of Proposition 73 are applicable in special elections and special runoff elections. Moreover, local contribution limits may be applicable. FACTS You serve on the Mendocino County Planning Commission as an appointed member and as an "at large" representative. You are interested in serving on a host committee and also soliciting funds for a candidate for the position of California Secretary of State in the November election. ANALYSIS In 1983, Section 84308 was added to the Act. The purpose for the provision was to ensure that appointed members of boards or commissions would not be biased by large campaign contributors or potential contributors who might appear before them in a proceeding involving a license, permit or entitlement for use. Section 84308 applies to all appointed officers of any state agency or local
